title: Dravya Kṣetra Kāla Bhāva (The Four-Fold Scope Declaration)
summary: Before taking the sāmāyika (vow of equanimity), the practitioner declares the exact 'boundaries' within which the vow will operate. This single declaration sets four limits — what things (dravya) one may keep with oneself, what place (kṣetra) one will stay within, how long (kāla) the vow lasts, and in what inner state (bhāva) one will hold it — and then resolves to keep the sāmāyika 'in six ways' (chha koṭi). It is recited so the vow is honest and precise: the practitioner promises only what can truly be kept, with full awareness (jayaṇā, careful restraint) about everything else.
ritualPlacement: In the Daivasika (Devasi) flow this declaration comes in the run-up to the sāmāyika proper: after Aḍhāra Pāpasthānaka and immediately before Karemi Bhante (the actual sāmāyika vow, the 1st Āvaśyaka), to which it stands as a preamble fixing the vow's scope. It is therefore not itself one of the six Āvaśyakas but a vidhi-sutra (procedural declaration) that frames the 1st Āvaśyaka. The same declaration is reused in the Deśāvagāśika (the 10th lay vow) observance, which adapts the Devasi sāmāyika procedure by word-substitution rather than printing fresh text. Note the closing rubric: the practitioner stands, declares the sāmāyika 'in six ways' (chha koṭi), and recites one Navkar to begin.
authorAndSource: This is a vernacular ritual declaration (in classical Gujarati prose, not a Prakrit gāthā) of the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa Gaccha) tradition, as printed in the authoritative book Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahita (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipakṣa edition). No individual author is named for it; it is traditional vidhi text. —
structureArc: A single prose unit (v.1) that proceeds through the four scope-factors in fixed order: (a) dravya — permitted possessions during the vow (clothes, beads, ornaments, bundle, mat, navkārvālī rosary), held only 'as resolved' (dhāryā pramāṇe); (b) kṣetra — the spatial limit, within the doorway of the upāśraya / the present place, with jayaṇā for necessity; (c) kāla — the temporal limit, lasting until the sāmāyika is completed; (d) bhāva — the inner limit, kept to one's capacity (yathāśakti), free of attachment and aversion (rāga-dveṣa), with the stated āgāra (exception) permitting speech with fellow vow-keepers and elders/gurus, and the pratyākhyāna (renunciation) — or jayaṇā — regarding speech with non-vow-keepers. The unit then closes with the resolve to keep the sāmāyika 'in six ways' (chha koṭi) and to stand and recite one Navkar to commence the vow.
keyThemes: Four-fold scoping of a vow (dravya / kṣetra / kāla / bhāva)
Jayaṇā — careful restraint where total abstention is impractical
Freedom from rāga and dveṣa as the inner aim of sāmāyika

glossary.definition: Substance / material things; here, the physical items a practitioner may keep during the vow, only as previously resolved.
Place / region; the spatial boundary within which the vow is kept (inside the doorway of the upāśraya or present place).
Time; the temporal limit of the vow, lasting until the sāmāyika is fulfilled.
Inner state / disposition; the mental scope of the vow, held to one's capacity and free of attachment and aversion.
The vow of equanimity (the 1st Āvaśyaka), a fixed period (traditionally 48 minutes) of stilled, non-violent, equanimous conduct.
The 'six ways' in which the sāmāyika is undertaken — not doing, not causing, not approving wrongful acts, across mind, speech and body.
Careful vigilance / restraint exercised when complete avoidance is impractical, so as to minimize harm.
'According to one's capacity'; the principle that one vows honestly to what one can actually sustain.
A declared, permitted exception within a vow that does not break it (e.g., speaking with fellow vow-keepers or gurus).
Renunciation / formal abstention vow (the 6th Āvaśyaka in general); here, the renunciation of speech with non-vow-keepers.
Attachment and aversion — the two passions the practitioner resolves to remain free of during the vow.
The rosary used for counting Navkar recitations, named among the permitted possessions (dravya).
The hall / place of religious observance whose threshold defines the spatial (kṣetra) limit of the vow.
A procedural ritual text (a declaration or stage-direction) as opposed to a verse-sūtra given the full word-by-word treatment.

howToRead: New learners should read this as a simple, honest 'terms of the vow' checklist — what I keep, where I stay, how long, and in what spirit — recited just before beginning sāmāyika. Advanced practitioners will recognize the classical four-factor (dravya/kṣetra/kāla/bhāva) scoping joined to the chha-koṭi formula, and the precise interplay of āgāra, pratyākhyāna, and jayaṇā that calibrates the vow to lived circumstance.

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahita (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipakṣa edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse text, ritual placement, and the Devasi/Deśāvagāśika reuse of the sāmāyika procedure.

sources.title: Padmanabh S. Jaini, The Jaina Path of Purification
sources.consultedFor: Doctrine of the sāmāyika vow and the dravya/kṣetra/kāla/bhāva scoping of vows.

notes: The book is in classical Gujarati prose, so this is a vernacular vidhi declaration rather than a Prakrit/Sanskrit gāthā; it receives an idiomatic translation plus plain explanation, not the full word-by-word verse treatment. The exact enumeration of the 'six koṭi' (chha koṭi) is not spelled out in this sutra's text itself; the common tradition (do not / cause not / approve not × mind-speech-body, i.e. 3 yoga × 2 karaṇa) is given as the standard reading and should be flagged as traditional rather than text-internal. The asterisked clause regarding speech with non-vow-keepers reflects an in-book footnote/variant (āgāra vs. pratyākhyāna vs. jayaṇā) preserved verbatim; treat the variant as attested, not as an error to normalize. URL for the source book is omitted as no stable verified link is available.
